🧠 Brainstorming directions

  1. ⚖gradingscheme Write the grading scheme. For each of the four proof parts, enumerate the load-bearing steps a correct answer must contain so a grader (human or structured) can check them one by one.
    show example
    Q1.1: (a) cites first-order optimality of T; (b) uses gamma-strong-convexity lower bound; (c) bounds gradient gap by beta * W_1; (d) applies epsilon-sensitivity to get kappa = epsilon*beta/gamma.
  2. ⚙problemsetup Fill in the problemsetup and problemtags metadata (title, domain, subtopic, contact, and the four tag axes).
    show example
    {Performative Retraining: Contraction and Asymptotic Normality}{Statistics}{Asymptotic theory / performative prediction}{Qualifying-exam-plus}{Zhixian Zhang}{zhixian.zhang@rutgers.edu}{Draft}
  3. 📓editornotes Complete the review sidecar: it is still the blank template. Paste the five raw model responses and write the failure-mode summary so Stage-1 can confirm the problem actually stumps frontier models.
  4. 🔑answerkey Consider extracting the single closed-form deliverable (the iteration-count bound t >= ln(||theta_0 - theta*||/delta) / ln(1/kappa)) as a separately exact-matchable sub-answer to give the verifier at least one deterministic hook.
💭 What's promising
  • Genuinely research-grade setup: a contraction argument plus an asymptotic-normality chain — exactly the kind of thing frontier models trip on.
  • The Question 1 answer key is rigorous and clean (strong-convexity / Kantorovich-Rubinstein / epsilon-sensitivity).
❓ Open questions to think through
  • The grading scheme is empty — a four-part proof cannot be graded without enumerated structural criteria.
  • All problemsetup / problemtags fields are still TODO, and the review sidecar is the blank template (no sample responses, no failure-mode summary).
🌱 Directions to explore
  • Write the grading scheme: enumerate the load-bearing steps each of the four proof parts must contain.
  • Fill in the metadata (title, domain, subtopic, contact, four tag axes).
  • Complete the sidecar with five raw model responses and a failure-mode summary so the hardness claim can be confirmed.
  • Consider extracting the closed-form iteration-count bound as a separately exact-matchable sub-answer.
Reviewed by in-session review agent · 2026-06-01 04:55 UTC · content sha 7aa2a463 .tex snapshot embedded above
#08

Travel Budget Problem

📄 Submission snapshot — parsed source evidence (private bank, unreleased; do not redistribute)
Jen decides to travel to 3 different countries. He spend \$400nit: Grammar: 'He spend' → 'He spends'. Also 'Jen' then 'He' — pick one gender/pronoun. for each country on the needed supplies. The tickets for travel cost, in total, 50\% more than the supplies.ambiguity: This sentence defines the TICKETS as 1.5x supplies = $1,800. It does not say 'total travel cost = supplies + tickets'. All five models read it the natural way. How much does the travel cost?ambiguity: 'The travel cost' has no antecedent other than the tickets just introduced; reading it as tickets+supplies is the contributor's interpretation, not a forced one.

Hover (desktop) or tap (touch) a highlighted span for reviewer's comment. Kind: strong suggestion ambiguity nit missing

Oracle (answer key) — the contributor's reference solution this review is checked against.

He spends \$400 for each country's supplies, and he visits 3 countries. So the total cost of supplies is $400 \times 3 = \$1,200$. The tickets for travel cost 50\% more than the supplies. So the travel cost is $1,200 + 1,200 + 1,200 \times 50\% = 1,200 \times 2.5 = \$3,000$.

Rubric (grading scheme) — how a verifier is meant to grade an answer.

Use a checklist for this question with the exact numerical answer. \begin{itemize} \item Exact expected answer is 3,000. \item The answer must be exactly 3,000 without any numerical tolerance. \item The integer 3,000, 3000 and expression $\$3,000, \$3000$ are acceptable. \end{itemize}
raw .tex source (provenance — verbatim, unparsed)

The full unparsed .tex as it was on master at review time (content sha 3c900085) — kept for provenance only; the parsed blocks above are the evidence to read.

\documentclass[../../../main.tex]{subfiles}
\begin{document}

% Keep this file in sync with the matching problem_08_review.md sidecar.

\problemsetup
  {Travel Budget Problem}
  {Mathematics}
  {Arithmetic}
  {Medium}
  {Zhixian Zhang}
  {zhixian.zhang@rutgers.edu}
  {Draft}
\problemtags
  {compute}
  {numerical reasoning}
  {exact match}
  {misunderstanding the question}
\begin{msbproblem}

\begin{problemstatement}
Jen decides to travel to 3 different countries. He spend \$400 for each country on the needed supplies. The tickets for travel cost, in total, 50\% more than the supplies. How much does the travel cost?
\end{problemstatement}

\begin{answerkey}
He spends \$400 for each country's supplies, and he visits 3 countries.
So the total cost of supplies is $400 \times 3 = \$1,200$.
The tickets for travel cost 50\% more than the supplies.
So the travel cost is $1,200 + 1,200 + 1,200 \times 50\% = 1,200 \times 2.5 = \$3,000$.
\end{answerkey}

\begin{gradingscheme}

Use a checklist for this question with the exact numerical answer.
\begin{itemize}
\item Exact expected answer is 3,000.
\item The answer must be exactly 3,000 without any numerical tolerance.
\item The integer 3,000, 3000 and expression $\$3,000, \$3000$ are acceptable.
\end{itemize}
\end{gradingscheme}

\begin{editornotes}
Suggested checks:
\begin{itemize}
\item Is every symbol defined?
\item Does the rubric use exact answer / tolerance for objective questions or a structural / judge rubric for proof-style questions?
\item Can the verifier grade deterministically, or at least be audited line by line from the written schema?
\item Does the problem require non-routine reasoning?
\item Does the matching review sidecar contain five raw model responses and a failure-mode summary?
\end{itemize}
\end{editornotes}

\end{msbproblem}

\end{document}
🏷 Proposed tags (agent — author to confirm)

These are agent-proposed tags (suggestions, not verdicts) to help the author — the human author / co-PI confirms or overrides. Grounded in the problem statement on master; not written into your .tex scaffold. Vocabulary from the working-hypothesis taxonomy (agents/taxonomy_curator/seed_taxonomy.yaml). Proposed 2026-06-09.

Capabilitycompute
Domainmath-other
SubtopicArithmetic word problem (percentage increase on a total)
Techniquetotal supplies = per-country cost * number of countries; apply '50% more than supplies' as 1.5x the supplies total (oracle reads tickets = 2.5x supplies). NOTE (info, not verdict): the oracle's '1200 + 1200 + 1200*50% = 1200*2.5' line conflicts with the plain reading '50% more = 1.5x = $1,800' — flag for author to confirm the intended answer (3000 vs 1800).
DifficultyBasic (label says Medium; routine percentage arithmetic, with a possible spec/oracle ambiguity)
Challenged capabilitynumerical reasoning, trap recognition
Grading modeexact
Observed failure modemisunderstanding the question (per contributor tag)

Agent-proposed structured tags (author/co-PI to confirm). Suggestions, not verdicts. Grounded in the problem statement + answer key on master; NOT written into the contributor's .tex scaffold. Vocabulary from agents/taxonomy_curator/seed_taxonomy.yaml (D1 #11, D2 #111).

📊 How the frontier models do — results matrix · verifier-graded

Sweep sweep-20260603-170524 · objective signal — information for humans to adjudicate.

⚖ author oracle ≠ model consensus (models→1800 vs oracle→3000) → needs oracle adjudication: is the oracle right, or do the frontier models reveal a real spec issue?

🔍 Resolved: the split turns on “the tickets for travel … How much does the travel cost”.
Oracle reads: whole trip: supplies 1200 + tickets 1800 = 3000
Models read: the tickets = 1800 (literal “travel”)
Status: ambiguous (leans models)

🧱 stumped 12/15 model×setup combos

model \ thinking setupS0 · no thinkingS1 · some thinkingS2 · max thinkingS3 · thinking + web
GPT-5.5≠ 1800×5≠ 1800×5≠ 1800×5—
Claude Opus 4.7= 3000×3—⚠ no answer—
GPT-5.4≠ 1800×3—≠ 1800×3—
Claude Opus 4.6= 3000×3—= 3000×3—
Claude Sonnet 4.6≠ 1800×5—≠ 1800×5≠ 1800×5
Claude Haiku 4.5≠ 1800×10≠ 1800×10≠ 1800×10≠ 1800×10

cell key: = matches oracle · ≠ differs · ? no oracle · ⚠ no answer · — not run · ×k = run count · click a cell for its full traces

⚠ Opus 4.7 coverage: no-thinking (S0) runs only — its thinking runs all errored in the harness (not model behavior); "no answer" cells there mean UNTESTED, not stumped. Re-run pending.

🧩 Failure / success-mode synthesis — how the models miss (and where they don’t)

Synthesized from the sweep (85 runs) + captured traces. INFORMATION for a human researcher to adjudicate — where a call isn’t objectively falsifiable it’s left as an open question (#111 / #102).

  • Partial success: the oracle 3000 was reached in 9/85 runs (11%) — by claude-opus-4-6, claude-opus-4-7. So it is reachable, just not reliably; the rest of the field lands elsewhere (see common wrong answers).
  • Common wrong answers: 1800×76. The most-given non-oracle answer is 1800 (76 runs) — a clustered wrong answer usually means one shared misstep, not random error.
  • The shared misstep (verbatim): the consensus answer 1800 comes from this reading —
    … either. Let me re-read once more. The statement "The tickets for travel cost, in total, 50% more than the supplies" directly defines what the tickets cost. Since supplies total $1,200, the tickets equal $1,200 × 1.5 = $1,800. The question asks for the travel cost, which refers to these tickets, so the answer is $1,800.
  • Where the readings diverge: the split turns on “the tickets for travel … How much does the travel cost”. Oracle reads it as whole trip: supplies 1200 + tickets 1800 = 3000; the models read it as the tickets = 1800 (literal “travel”). Status: ambiguous (leans models).
  • More thinking moves AWAY from the oracle: oracle-hit rate 21% at low thinking → 12% at high (more thinking moves AWAY from the oracle). That is an unusual signal — extra reasoning entrenching the same wrong reading often means the stem is steering models, not that they are under-thinking.
  • Web-search did NOT rescue it: with web on, the modal answer is still 1800 (15/15), oracle hits 0 — searching did not move the field, a low-contamination signal.

Open questions (for a human — not verdicts):

  • ❓ The phrase is ambiguous (the models’ reading is defensible) — should the stem be disambiguated, or the oracle reconciled, before this counts as a model failure?
  • ❓ Does more thinking hurting (vs helping) point at an ambiguous stem rather than a genuine reasoning gap?
🧭 Trajectory analysis — cross-setup patterns (85 runs)

Mined from captured reasoning traces · objective signal — information for humans to adjudicate.

  • Consensus: models cluster on 1800 (76/85 runs, 89%). Tight agreement → either a shared blind spot or a clean answer.
  • Oracle reach: 3000 reached in 9/85 runs (11%) (reached only under S0_nothink, S2_think10k).
  • Thinking dose-response: hurts oracle-hit rate 21% at low thinking → 12% at high (more thinking moves AWAY from the oracle).
  • Web-search effect: with web on, modal answer 1800 (15/15), oracle hits 0 — searching did not shift the field (low contamination signal).

Where the trajectories fork (verbatim reasoning tail):

1800 · consensus answer (76 runs · claude-haiku-4-5 / S3_think_web)
… either. Let me re-read once more. The statement "The tickets for travel cost, in total, 50% more than the supplies" directly defines what the tickets cost. Since supplies total $1,200, the tickets equal $1,200 × 1.5 = $1,800. The question asks for the travel cost, which refers to these tickets, so the answer is $1,800.
3000 · oracle answer (9 runs · claude-opus-4-6 / S2_think10k)
Supplies: 3 countries × $400 = $1200 Tickets: $1200 + 50% × $1200 = $1200 × 1.5 = $1800 Total travel cost: $1200 + $1800 = $3000

response
## Solution **Step 1: Calculate total supplies cost.** $$\$400 \times 3 \text{ countries} = \$1{,}200$$ **Step 2: Calculate the ticket cost (50% more than supplies).** $$\$1{,}200 \times 1.5 = \$1{,}800$$ Final answer: **\$1,800**

Let me solve this step by step. Supplies cost: 3 countries × $400 = $1,200 Tickets cost 50% more than supplies: Tickets = $1,200 × 1.50 = $1,800
response
## Solution **Step 1: Calculate total supplies cost.** Jen travels to 3 countries and spends $400 per country on supplies: $$\text{Total supplies} = 3 \times \$400 = \$1{,}200$$ **Step 2: Calculate the ticket (travel) cost.** The tickets cost 50% *more* than the supplies: $$\text{Travel cost} = \$1{,}200 \times (1 + 0.50) = \$1{,}200 \times 1.5 = \$1{,}800$$ Final answer: **$1,800**

response
## Solution **Step 1: Calculate total supplies cost.** Jen travels to 3 countries, spending $400 per country: $$\text{Total supplies} = 3 \times \$400 = \$1{,}200$$ **Step 2: Calculate the travel (ticket) cost.** The tickets cost 50% more than the supplies: $$\text{Travel cost} = \$1{,}200 \times 1.50 = \$1{,}800$$ Final answer: **$1,800**

# Travel Cost Calculation ## Problem Jen decides to travel to 3 different countries. He spends $400 for each country on the needed supplies. The tickets for travel cost, in total, 50% more than the supplies. **Question:** How much does travel cost? ## Solution **Step 1:** Calculate total supplies cost - 3 × $400 = $1,200 **Step 2:** Calculate ticket cost (50% more than supplies) - $1,200 × 1.5 = $1,800 ## Answer The travel (tickets) costs **$1,800**.

Your failure-mode summary:

  • Observed failure mode: misunderstanding the question
  • Summary: The phrasing 'The tickets for travel cost, in total, 50% more than the supplies' serves as a subtle linguistic trap, as it actually implies that 'The tickets for travel cost 50% more than the supplies in total.' Consequently, the final inquiry for the 'travel cost' is a macro-concept that encompasses both the ticketing expenses and the prerequisite supplies, rather than isolating the tickets alone. The model's failure to calculate this aggregated total demonstrates a strict, superficial literalism—treating 'travel cost' as a narrow synonym for 'tickets' while ignoring the overarching financial context established by the modifier.

Generated Python verifier (213 lines) 
# === BEGIN inlined answer_extract ===
"""Shared answer-extraction + normalization for deterministic verifiers.

WHY this module exists (Yulin, D1 #48 / GOALS C4): the whole point of the
benchmark is to *fail* frontier models. A verifier that searches for the oracle
number ANYWHERE in the response is dangerously lax — e.g. a wrong response that
reasons "3 steaks × 6 = 18, + waffles 10 = 28" CONTAINS "10" and would be
falsely marked PASS, hiding a real model failure. So every numeric grader here
extracts the model's FINAL answer (boxed / "Final answer:" / last line) and
compares THAT to the oracle, never a loose substring scan.

Public API:
  extract_final_number(response)  -> float | None    (the model's final number)
  extract_all_finals(response)    -> list[float]      (final-zone numbers, dedup)
  numbers_equal(a, b, tol)        -> bool
  final_number_matches(response, oracle, tol) -> (bool, reason)

Designed to be imported by per-problem verifiers AND inlined (the verifier
sandbox runs a single .py file) — see `inline_source()`.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import re
from pathlib import Path


# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Normalization helpers
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

def _strip_latex(text: str) -> str:
    """Remove the LaTeX scaffolding that wraps numbers in model output."""
    t = text
    # \boxed{...} -> keep the inner content (handled separately for capture,
    # but for general normalization just drop the wrapper token)
    t = t.replace(r"\boxed", " ")
    t = t.replace(r"\text", " ")
    t = t.replace(r"\$", " ")
    t = re.sub(r"\\[a-zA-Z]+", " ", t)   # other LaTeX commands
    t = t.replace("{", " ").replace("}", " ")
    t = t.replace("$", " ")
    # LaTeX thin-space comma grouping: 225{,}000 already had braces dropped ->
    # "225 , 000"; the number regex below re-joins comma groups, but braces gone
    # leaves a stray comma+space. Collapse "<digit> , <digit>" -> "<digit>,<digit>".
    t = re.sub(r"(\d)\s*,\s*(\d{3}\b)", r"\1,\2", t)
    t = re.sub(r"(\d)\s+(\d{3}\b)", r"\1,\2", t)  # "225 000" grouping -> "225,000"
    return t


# A number token: optional sign, comma-grouped or plain integer, optional decimal.
_NUM_RE = re.compile(r"[-+]?\d{1,3}(?:,\d{3})+(?:\.\d+)?|[-+]?\d+(?:\.\d+)?")


def _to_float(tok: str) -> float | None:
    try:
        return float(tok.replace(",", ""))
    except (ValueError, AttributeError):
        return None


def parse_numbers(text: str) -> list[float]:
    """All numeric tokens in (normalized) text, in order, as floats."""
    t = _strip_latex(text)
    out = []
    for m in _NUM_RE.finditer(t):
        v = _to_float(m.group(0))
        if v is not None:
            out.append(v)
    return out


# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Final-answer extraction (the load-bearing part)
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Markers, in priority order, that introduce a model's committed final answer.
_FINAL_MARKERS = [
    r"final\s+answer\s*[:=]?",
    r"the\s+answer\s+is\s*[:=]?",
    r"answer\s*[:=]",
    r"\\boxed",
    r"\bboxed\b",
    r"therefore[,:]?",
    r"thus[,:]?",
    r"in\s+total[,:]?",
    r"total\s*(?:is|=|:)",
    r"hence[,:]?",
]


def _boxed_contents(response: str) -> list[float]:
    """Numbers inside \\boxed{...} — the strongest commitment signal."""
    out = []
    for m in re.finditer(r"\\?boxed\s*\{([^{}]*)\}", response):
        nums = parse_numbers(m.group(1))
        out.extend(nums)
    return out


def extract_final_number(response: str) -> float | None:
    """Return the single number the model committed to as its final answer.

    Strategy (most → least reliable):
      1. number inside the LAST \\boxed{...}
      2. number on/after the LAST line that contains a final-answer marker
      3. the last number in the last non-empty line
      4. the last number anywhere
    Returns None if no number can be found at all.
    """
    if not response or not response.strip():
        return None

    # 1. boxed
    boxed = _boxed_contents(response)
    if boxed:
        return boxed[-1]

    lines = [ln for ln in response.splitlines() if ln.strip()]

    # 2. last marker line
    marker_re = re.compile("|".join(_FINAL_MARKERS), re.IGNORECASE)
    for ln in reversed(lines):
        m = marker_re.search(ln)
        if m:
            tail = ln[m.start():]
            nums = parse_numbers(tail)
            if nums:
                return nums[-1]
            # marker but no number on this line -> look at the whole line
            nums = parse_numbers(ln)
            if nums:
                return nums[-1]

    # 3. last number in the last non-empty line
    if lines:
        nums = parse_numbers(lines[-1])
        if nums:
            return nums[-1]

    # 4. last number anywhere
    allnums = parse_numbers(response)
    return allnums[-1] if allnums else None


def extract_all_finals(response: str) -> list[float]:
    """Numbers appearing in the final-answer ZONE (boxed + every marker line +
    last line), de-duplicated, order-preserving. Used by disjunctive/structural
    graders (e.g. p5 "6 or 12") that must see the full set the model committed
    to, and reject extra spurious finals."""
    out: list[float] = []

    def _add(nums):
        for n in nums:
            if n not in out:
                out.append(n)

    _add(_boxed_contents(response))

    marker_re = re.compile("|".join(_FINAL_MARKERS), re.IGNORECASE)
    for ln in response.splitlines():
        if marker_re.search(ln):
            m = marker_re.search(ln)
            _add(parse_numbers(ln[m.start():]))

    lines = [ln for ln in response.splitlines() if ln.strip()]
    if lines:
        _add(parse_numbers(lines[-1]))
    return out


# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Comparison
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

def numbers_equal(a: float | None, b: float | None, tol: float = 0.0) -> bool:
    if a is None or b is None:
        return False
    if tol <= 0:
        # exact-match mode: integers compared exactly, allow trivial float noise
        return abs(a - b) < 1e-9
    return abs(a - b) <= tol


def final_number_matches(response: str, oracle: float,
                         tol: float = 0.0) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """Core grader: does the model's FINAL number equal the oracle?

    Returns (ok, reason). Reason always names what was extracted so the
    validation trace is auditable (Yulin: audit line-by-line)."""
    got = extract_final_number(response)
    if got is None:
        return False, "no numeric final answer could be extracted"
    if numbers_equal(got, oracle, tol):
        return True, f"final answer {_fmt(got)} == oracle {_fmt(oracle)}"
    return False, f"final answer {_fmt(got)} != oracle {_fmt(oracle)}"


def _fmt(x: float) -> str:
    if x == int(x):
        return str(int(x))
    return str(x)
# === END inlined answer_extract ===


def grade(response: str, context: dict) -> dict:
    """Travel Budget Problem

    Oracle: 3000 (author answerkey). Mode: exact final-answer match.
    Sweep dispute: '50% more than the supplies' is read by most as 1.5x$1200=$1800 (the cost OF the tickets); author reads 'travel cost'=supplies+tickets=2.5x$1200=$3000."""
    oracle = 3000
    tol = 0
    ok, reason = final_number_matches(response, oracle, tol)
    return {"pass": bool(ok), "score": 1.0 if ok else 0.0, "notes": reason}
Validation trace (4 checks)
verdictresponse (truncated)verifier notes
✓ rejectedSupplies $1200; tickets 50% more = $1800. Final answer: 1800final answer 1800 != oracle 3000
✓ rejectedTravel cost = 1200 * 1.5 = 1800. Final answer: $1,800final answer 1800 != oracle 3000
✓ rejectedThe travel costs $600. Final answer: 600final answer 600 != oracle 3000
✓ rejectedTotal is $1200. Final answer: 1200final answer 1200 != oracle 3000

— reviewer notes below are secondary (gap-filling suggestions, not a ruling) —

The 'failure' is a defensible reading: models answer $1,800 correctly for the sentence as written, and the oracle's own $3,000 algebra double-counts supplies.

🔧 Quick fixes & next steps

  1. 📝problemstatement Decide which answer the problem actually tests and rewrite so the text forces it. If the intended answer is $3,000 (total trip cost), state the deliverable explicitly as total cost.
    show example
    What is Jen's total cost for the trip (supplies plus tickets)?
  2. 🔑answerkey Fix the oracle algebra so it matches the chosen reading. As written, '50% more than the supplies' gives tickets = $1,800; total = supplies + tickets = $1,200 + $1,800 = $3,000. The line '1200 + 1200 + 1200*50%' should be rewritten as '1200 (supplies) + 1800 (tickets) = 3000'.
    show example
    Tickets = 1200 * 1.5 = $1,800. Total trip cost = 1200 + 1800 = $3,000.
  3. 📝problemstatement Reconsider whether this belongs in a hard-set at all: the only thing that stumps the models is a wording ambiguity, and the natural reading ($1,800) is arguably correct. A wording trap is not a reasoning failure. Consider replacing with a variant whose single correct answer survives a careful, literal read.
  4. ⚙problemsetup Update the difficulty label to Basic for the arithmetic content, or raise the actual cognitive load if you want Medium to hold.
✨ What's working
  • Self-contained and short — no external context needed.
  • Grading scheme pre-lists acceptable string forms, which is the right verifier-friendly instinct.
💡 Things to consider
  • 'The travel cost' has no antecedent but the tickets just named; reading it as tickets+supplies is one interpretation, not a forced one — all five models took the natural reading.
  • The oracle is internally inconsistent: '50% more than supplies' is $1,800, but the answer key reaches $3,000 by adding supplies a second time.
👉 Suggested next steps
  • Decide the intended answer and rewrite the question to force it (e.g. 'What is Jen's total trip cost, supplies plus tickets?').
  • Fix the answer-key algebra: tickets = 1200*1.5 = 1800; total = 1200 + 1800 = 3000.
  • Reconsider whether a wording-trap item belongs in a hard-set at all — the natural reading is arguably correct.
  • Relabel difficulty to Basic, or raise the actual cognitive load.
Reviewed by in-session review agent · 2026-06-01 04:55 UTC · content sha 3c900085 .tex snapshot embedded above
#09

Parallel Task Resource Allocation

📄 Submission snapshot — parsed source evidence (private bank, unreleased; do not redistribute)
Carla can cook a batch of waffles in 10 minutes and chicken-fried steak in 6 minutes. How long will it take her to cook 3 steaks and a batch of waffles together?ambiguity: Unstated: can she cook all 3 steaks at once (one 6-min batch) or only one at a time (3*6=18)? The oracle assumes a single simultaneous batch; Response 5's 18-minute answer follows the other reading and is defensible.

Hover (desktop) or tap (touch) a highlighted span for reviewer's comment. Kind: strong suggestion ambiguity nit missing

Oracle (answer key) — the contributor's reference solution this review is checked against.

Since Carla cooks the waffles and steaks together (at the same time), the tasks can be performed in parallel. Cooking a batch of waffles takes the longest amount of time, which is 10 minutes. The 3 steaks only require 6 minutes to cook and can be prepared simultaneously within that 10-minute window. Therefore, the total time required is determined by the longest duration, which is 10 minutes.

Rubric (grading scheme) — how a verifier is meant to grade an answer.

Use a checklist for this question with the exact numerical answer. \begin{itemize} \item Exact expected answer is 10 minutes. \item The answer must be exactly 10 without any numerical tolerance. \item The answer 28 minutes or 18 minutes are unacceptable. \end{itemize}
raw .tex source (provenance — verbatim, unparsed)

The full unparsed .tex as it was on master at review time (content sha a937a336) — kept for provenance only; the parsed blocks above are the evidence to read.

\documentclass[../../../main.tex]{subfiles}
\begin{document}

% Keep this file in sync with the matching problem_09_review.md sidecar.

\problemsetup
  {Parallel Task Resource Allocation}
  {Mathematics}
  {Arithmetic}
  {Medium}
  {Zhixian Zhang}
  {zhixian.zhang@rutgers.edu}
  {Draft}
\problemtags
  {compute, diagnose}
  {Numerical reasoning}
  {exact match}
  {missing the word 'together'}
\begin{msbproblem}

\begin{problemstatement}
Carla can cook a batch of waffles in 10 minutes and chicken-fried steak in 6 minutes. How long will it take her to cook 3 steaks and a batch of waffles together?
\end{problemstatement}

\begin{answerkey}
Since Carla cooks the waffles and steaks together (at the same time), the tasks can be performed in parallel.
Cooking a batch of waffles takes the longest amount of time, which is 10 minutes.
The 3 steaks only require 6 minutes to cook and can be prepared simultaneously within that 10-minute window.
Therefore, the total time required is determined by the longest duration, which is 10 minutes.
\end{answerkey}

\begin{gradingscheme}

Use a checklist for this question with the exact numerical answer.
\begin{itemize}
\item Exact expected answer is 10 minutes.
\item The answer must be exactly 10 without any numerical tolerance.
\item The answer 28 minutes or 18 minutes are unacceptable.
\end{itemize}

\end{gradingscheme}

\begin{editornotes}
Suggested checks:
\begin{itemize}
\item Is every symbol defined?
\item Does the rubric use exact answer / tolerance for objective questions or a structural / judge rubric for proof-style questions?
\item Can the verifier grade deterministically, or at least be audited line by line from the written schema?
\item Does the problem require non-routine reasoning?
\item Does the matching review sidecar contain five raw model responses and a failure-mode summary?
\end{itemize}
\end{editornotes}

\end{msbproblem}

\end{document}

DifficultyBasic-to-Intermediate (label says Medium; the trap is parallel-vs-sequential reading of 'together')

Agent-proposed structured tags (author/co-PI to confirm). Suggestions, not verdicts. Grounded in the problem statement + answer key on master; NOT written into the contributor's .tex scaffold. Vocabulary from agents/taxonomy_curator/seed_taxonomy.yaml (D1 #11, D2 #111).

📊 How the frontier models do — results matrix · verifier-graded

Sweep sweep-20260603-170524 · objective signal — information for humans to adjudicate.

⚖ author oracle ≠ model consensus (models→28 vs oracle→10) → needs oracle adjudication: is the oracle right, or do the frontier models reveal a real spec issue?

🔍 Resolved: the split turns on “cook 3 steaks and a batch of waffles together”.
Oracle reads: full parallel (3 steaks at once 6m, waffles 10m) = 10
Models read: serial sum 3×6+10 = 28
Status: ambiguous (degree of parallelism; steaks-serial/waffles-parallel = 18)

🧱 stumped 12/15 model×setup combos

cell key: = matches oracle · ≠ differs · ? no oracle · ⚠ no answer · — not run · ×k = run count · click a cell for its full traces

⚠ Opus 4.7 coverage: no-thinking (S0) runs only — its thinking runs all errored in the harness (not model behavior); "no answer" cells there mean UNTESTED, not stumped. Re-run pending.

🧩 Failure / success-mode synthesis — how the models miss (and where they don’t)

Synthesized from the sweep (85 runs) + captured traces. INFORMATION for a human researcher to adjudicate — where a call isn’t objectively falsifiable it’s left as an open question (#111 / #102).

  • Partial success: the oracle 10 was reached in 7/85 runs (8%) — by claude-haiku-4-5. So it is reachable, just not reliably; the rest of the field lands elsewhere (see common wrong answers).
  • Common wrong answers: 28×53 · 18×25. The most-given non-oracle answer is 28 (53 runs) — a clustered wrong answer usually means one shared misstep, not random error.
  • The shared misstep (verbatim): the consensus answer 28 comes from this reading —
    … utes. Though reconsidering the phrasing, a sequential interpretation might be more standard for a word problem. If cooking one after another: 3 steaks at 6 minutes each equals 18 minutes, plus 10 minutes for waffles, totaling 28 minutes. This sequential approach seems like the most straightforward mathematical reading.
  • Where the readings diverge: the split turns on “cook 3 steaks and a batch of waffles together”. Oracle reads it as full parallel (3 steaks at once 6m, waffles 10m) = 10; the models read it as serial sum 3×6+10 = 28. Status: ambiguous (degree of parallelism; steaks-serial/waffles-parallel = 18).
  • Thinking helps: oracle-hit rate 0% at low thinking → 8% at high (more thinking moves toward the oracle) — consistent with a problem that is hard-but-solvable with more reasoning budget.
  • Web-search rescue: with web on, the oracle was hit 1/15 times — search shifted the field toward the oracle, a possible contamination / look-up-ability signal.
  • Red flag — a small model reached the oracle: claude-haiku-4-5 hit 10. The bar is to fail frontier models; a small/cheap model solving it is a sign the problem may be too easy or look-up-able (D1 #48).

Open questions (for a human — not verdicts):

  • ❓ The phrase is ambiguous (the models’ reading is defensible) — should the stem be disambiguated, or the oracle reconciled, before this counts as a model failure?
  • ❓ Web-search reaching the oracle when offline runs don’t — is the answer look-up-able (contamination risk), or did search just supply a missing fact?
  • ❓ A small model reached the oracle — does this problem still stump the frontier models it is meant to, or is it too easy / contaminated?
🧭 Trajectory analysis — cross-setup patterns (85 runs)

Mined from captured reasoning traces · objective signal — information for humans to adjudicate.

  • Consensus: models cluster on 28 (53/85 runs, 62%). Tight agreement → either a shared blind spot or a clean answer.
  • Oracle reach: 10 reached in 7/85 runs (8%) (reached only under S1_think4k, S2_think10k, S3_think_web).
  • Thinking dose-response: helps oracle-hit rate 0% at low thinking → 8% at high (more thinking moves toward the oracle).
  • Web-search effect: with web on, modal answer 28 (7/15), oracle hits 1 — searching did not shift the field (low contamination signal).

Where the trajectories fork (verbatim reasoning tail):

28 · consensus answer (53 runs · claude-haiku-4-5 / S3_think_web)
… utes. Though reconsidering the phrasing, a sequential interpretation might be more standard for a word problem. If cooking one after another: 3 steaks at 6 minutes each equals 18 minutes, plus 10 minutes for waffles, totaling 28 minutes. This sequential approach seems like the most straightforward mathematical reading.
10 · oracle answer (7 runs · claude-haiku-4-5 / S2_think10k)
… same time, so the total time is limited by whichever takes longest. Since waffles need 10 minutes and steaks need 6, the answer would be 10 minutes. I briefly entertained a more complex scenario where I'd cook some items in batches, but that doesn't align with the straightforward reading of cooking everything together.

response
Carla can cook multiple items simultaneously, so the total time is determined by the longest single task... but wait, she needs to cook 3 steaks and 1 batch of waffles. If she can only cook one item at a time: - 3 steaks × 6 minutes = 18 minutes - 1 batch of waffles = 10 minutes - Total = 28 minutes But if she can cook them simultaneously (in parallel), the answer would be different. However, the problem says "together" which likely means at the same time where possible, but she's one person with presumably one cooking station per item. The most straightforward interpretation: she cooks them sequentially. - 3 steaks: 3 × 6 = 18 minutes - 1 batch of waffles: 10 minutes - Total: 18 + 10 = 28 minutes Final answer: 28 minutes
S0_nothink k118 ·103/962.9s · $0.0029
trace
response
Carla can cook multiple items simultaneously (together means at the same time). - Waffles take 10 minutes - Each steak takes 6 minutes, so 3 steaks take 3 × 6 = 18 minutes Cooking them together means she works on them simultaneously, so the total time is the maximum of the two: 18 minutes. Final answer: 18 minutes
S0_nothink k228 ·103/2375.6s · $0.0064
trace
response
Carla can cook multiple items simultaneously (together means at the same time). - Waffles take 10 minutes - Each steak takes 6 minutes, but 3 steaks can be cooked simultaneously (or sequentially) Wait - "cook 3 steaks and a batch of waffles together" - I need to consider whether she cooks steaks sequentially or simultaneously. If she cooks one steak at a time: 3 steaks × 6 minutes = 18 minutes for steaks, and waffles take 10 minutes. If she does them all together (overlapping), the total time is the maximum... but she can only cook one thing at a time presumably. Most likely interpretation: she cooks items one at a time sequentially. - 3 steaks: 3 × 6 = 18 minutes - 1 batch of waffles: 10 minutes - Total: 18 + 10 = 28 minutes Final answer: 28 minutes
S0_nothink k318 ·103/1814.7s · $0.0050
trace
response
Carla can cook multiple items simultaneously, so the total time is determined by the longest single item or the sequence required. Since she's cooking them "together" (simultaneously), the time is the maximum of the two cooking times... but she has 3 steaks. If she can cook one steak at a time (sequentially): 3 steaks × 6 minutes = 18 minutes, plus waffles take 10 minutes. If she cooks the waffles simultaneously with the steaks, she can start the waffles while cooking steaks. The steaks take 18 minutes total, and waffles take 10 minutes, so she can finish the waffles within the 18-minute window. Total time = 18 minutes. Final answer: 18 minutes
S0_nothink k428 ·103/2235.4s · $0.0061
trace
response

She needs to cook 3 steaks and 1 batch of waffles. If she can only cook one item at a time: - 3 steaks × 6 minutes = 18 minutes - 1 batch of waffles = 10 minutes - Total = 28 minutes But if she can cook them simultaneously (overlap), she would start all items at once and finish when the last one is done. However, she likely can only do one thing at a time. The most natural interpretation: she cooks them sequentially. - 3 steaks: 3 × 6 = 18 minutes - 1 batch of waffles: 10 minutes - Total: 18 + 10 = 28 minutes Final answer: 28 minutes

# Solution Looking at this problem, I need to determine whether Carla cooks these items sequentially or in parallel. The word "together" in the problem suggests she's cooking everything simultaneously (like steaks on one burner and waffles on another), so the total time would be determined by whichever takes longest. **Individual cooking times:** - 3 steaks at 6 minutes each = 3 × 6 = 18 minutes - 1 batch of waffles = 10 minutes **Assuming parallel cooking** (steaks cooking on one surface, waffles on another): - The steaks finish after 18 minutes - The waffles finish after 10 minutes - Total time = max(18, 10) = **18 minutes** The steaks are still being cooked when the waffles are done, so we wait for the steaks to finish. Final answer: 18 minutes

Total time = max(6 minutes, 10 minutes) = **10 minutes** Alternatively, if the steaks must be cooked sequentially (one at a time): - 3 steaks × 6 minutes = 18 minutes - 1 batch of waffles = 10 minutes - Total = 28 minutes Given the word "together" in the problem, the simultaneous interpretation is more likely. **Final answer: 10 minutes**

Generated Python verifier (213 lines) 
# === BEGIN inlined answer_extract ===
"""Shared answer-extraction + normalization for deterministic verifiers.

WHY this module exists (Yulin, D1 #48 / GOALS C4): the whole point of the
benchmark is to *fail* frontier models. A verifier that searches for the oracle
number ANYWHERE in the response is dangerously lax — e.g. a wrong response that
reasons "3 steaks × 6 = 18, + waffles 10 = 28" CONTAINS "10" and would be
falsely marked PASS, hiding a real model failure. So every numeric grader here
extracts the model's FINAL answer (boxed / "Final answer:" / last line) and
compares THAT to the oracle, never a loose substring scan.

Public API:
  extract_final_number(response)  -> float | None    (the model's final number)
  extract_all_finals(response)    -> list[float]      (final-zone numbers, dedup)
  numbers_equal(a, b, tol)        -> bool
  final_number_matches(response, oracle, tol) -> (bool, reason)

Designed to be imported by per-problem verifiers AND inlined (the verifier
sandbox runs a single .py file) — see `inline_source()`.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import re
from pathlib import Path


# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Normalization helpers
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

def _strip_latex(text: str) -> str:
    """Remove the LaTeX scaffolding that wraps numbers in model output."""
    t = text
    # \boxed{...} -> keep the inner content (handled separately for capture,
    # but for general normalization just drop the wrapper token)
    t = t.replace(r"\boxed", " ")
    t = t.replace(r"\text", " ")
    t = t.replace(r"\$", " ")
    t = re.sub(r"\\[a-zA-Z]+", " ", t)   # other LaTeX commands
    t = t.replace("{", " ").replace("}", " ")
    t = t.replace("$", " ")
    # LaTeX thin-space comma grouping: 225{,}000 already had braces dropped ->
    # "225 , 000"; the number regex below re-joins comma groups, but braces gone
    # leaves a stray comma+space. Collapse "<digit> , <digit>" -> "<digit>,<digit>".
    t = re.sub(r"(\d)\s*,\s*(\d{3}\b)", r"\1,\2", t)
    t = re.sub(r"(\d)\s+(\d{3}\b)", r"\1,\2", t)  # "225 000" grouping -> "225,000"
    return t


# A number token: optional sign, comma-grouped or plain integer, optional decimal.
_NUM_RE = re.compile(r"[-+]?\d{1,3}(?:,\d{3})+(?:\.\d+)?|[-+]?\d+(?:\.\d+)?")


def _to_float(tok: str) -> float | None:
    try:
        return float(tok.replace(",", ""))
    except (ValueError, AttributeError):
        return None


def parse_numbers(text: str) -> list[float]:
    """All numeric tokens in (normalized) text, in order, as floats."""
    t = _strip_latex(text)
    out = []
    for m in _NUM_RE.finditer(t):
        v = _to_float(m.group(0))
        if v is not None:
            out.append(v)
    return out


# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Final-answer extraction (the load-bearing part)
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Markers, in priority order, that introduce a model's committed final answer.
_FINAL_MARKERS = [
    r"final\s+answer\s*[:=]?",
    r"the\s+answer\s+is\s*[:=]?",
    r"answer\s*[:=]",
    r"\\boxed",
    r"\bboxed\b",
    r"therefore[,:]?",
    r"thus[,:]?",
    r"in\s+total[,:]?",
    r"total\s*(?:is|=|:)",
    r"hence[,:]?",
]


def _boxed_contents(response: str) -> list[float]:
    """Numbers inside \\boxed{...} — the strongest commitment signal."""
    out = []
    for m in re.finditer(r"\\?boxed\s*\{([^{}]*)\}", response):
        nums = parse_numbers(m.group(1))
        out.extend(nums)
    return out


def extract_final_number(response: str) -> float | None:
    """Return the single number the model committed to as its final answer.

    Strategy (most → least reliable):
      1. number inside the LAST \\boxed{...}
      2. number on/after the LAST line that contains a final-answer marker
      3. the last number in the last non-empty line
      4. the last number anywhere
    Returns None if no number can be found at all.
    """
    if not response or not response.strip():
        return None

    # 1. boxed
    boxed = _boxed_contents(response)
    if boxed:
        return boxed[-1]

    lines = [ln for ln in response.splitlines() if ln.strip()]

    # 2. last marker line
    marker_re = re.compile("|".join(_FINAL_MARKERS), re.IGNORECASE)
    for ln in reversed(lines):
        m = marker_re.search(ln)
        if m:
            tail = ln[m.start():]
            nums = parse_numbers(tail)
            if nums:
                return nums[-1]
            # marker but no number on this line -> look at the whole line
            nums = parse_numbers(ln)
            if nums:
                return nums[-1]

    # 3. last number in the last non-empty line
    if lines:
        nums = parse_numbers(lines[-1])
        if nums:
            return nums[-1]

    # 4. last number anywhere
    allnums = parse_numbers(response)
    return allnums[-1] if allnums else None


def extract_all_finals(response: str) -> list[float]:
    """Numbers appearing in the final-answer ZONE (boxed + every marker line +
    last line), de-duplicated, order-preserving. Used by disjunctive/structural
    graders (e.g. p5 "6 or 12") that must see the full set the model committed
    to, and reject extra spurious finals."""
    out: list[float] = []

    def _add(nums):
        for n in nums:
            if n not in out:
                out.append(n)

    _add(_boxed_contents(response))

    marker_re = re.compile("|".join(_FINAL_MARKERS), re.IGNORECASE)
    for ln in response.splitlines():
        if marker_re.search(ln):
            m = marker_re.search(ln)
            _add(parse_numbers(ln[m.start():]))

    lines = [ln for ln in response.splitlines() if ln.strip()]
    if lines:
        _add(parse_numbers(lines[-1]))
    return out


# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Comparison
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

def numbers_equal(a: float | None, b: float | None, tol: float = 0.0) -> bool:
    if a is None or b is None:
        return False
    if tol <= 0:
        # exact-match mode: integers compared exactly, allow trivial float noise
        return abs(a - b) < 1e-9
    return abs(a - b) <= tol


def final_number_matches(response: str, oracle: float,
                         tol: float = 0.0) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """Core grader: does the model's FINAL number equal the oracle?

    Returns (ok, reason). Reason always names what was extracted so the
    validation trace is auditable (Yulin: audit line-by-line)."""
    got = extract_final_number(response)
    if got is None:
        return False, "no numeric final answer could be extracted"
    if numbers_equal(got, oracle, tol):
        return True, f"final answer {_fmt(got)} == oracle {_fmt(oracle)}"
    return False, f"final answer {_fmt(got)} != oracle {_fmt(oracle)}"


def _fmt(x: float) -> str:
    if x == int(x):
        return str(int(x))
    return str(x)
# === END inlined answer_extract ===


def grade(response: str, context: dict) -> dict:
    """Parallel Task Resource Allocation

    Oracle: 10 (author answerkey). Mode: exact final-answer match.
    Sweep dispute: 'together' signals parallel (author oracle 10 = max(6,10)); most models read serial -> 3*6+10=28, or steaks-serial -> 18."""
    oracle = 10
    tol = 0
    ok, reason = final_number_matches(response, oracle, tol)
    return {"pass": bool(ok), "score": 1.0 if ok else 0.0, "notes": reason}
Validation trace (4 checks)
verdictresponse (truncated)verifier notes
✓ rejected3 steaks x 6 = 18 min, + waffles 10 min, total 28 min. Final answer: 28 minutesfinal answer 28 != oracle 10
✓ rejectedSteaks 18 min then waffles overlap, finishes at 18. Final answer: 18 minutesfinal answer 18 != oracle 10
✓ rejectedIt takes 16 minutes. Final answer: 16final answer 16 != oracle 10
✓ rejectedTotal time is 6 minutes. Final answer: 6 minutesfinal answer 6 != oracle 10

— reviewer notes below are secondary (gap-filling suggestions, not a ruling) —

Real, consistent anti-memorization failure (4/5 said 28), but a steak-batch ambiguity makes the rival answer 18 defensible — pin it down.

🔧 Quick fixes & next steps

  1. 📝problemstatement Close the steak-batch ambiguity in the problem statement so 10 is unambiguously correct. Specify that all steaks can cook at the same time (e.g. 'a single skillet that holds all the steaks at once').
    show example
    Carla can cook a batch of waffles in 10 minutes, and she can cook all her steaks in one 6-minute batch. If she cooks the 3 steaks and the waffles at the same time, how long will it take?
  2. ⚖gradingscheme Expand the grading scheme to explicitly reject 18 as well as 28, and to note WHY each wrong answer arises (18 = treated steaks as a sequential 3*6 sum without parallelism vs waffles; 28 = full sequential sum).
    show example
    Reject 28 (sequential sum of all tasks) and 18 (sequential steaks); accept only 10 (parallel: max of the concurrent task durations).
  3. 📓editornotes Note the contamination angle in the editor notes: the base item is a known GSM8K problem, so the hardness depends entirely on the 'together' perturbation surviving. If a model is prompted to read carefully, it may recover — worth a higher-k re-run.
✨ What's working
  • Clear failure signal: four of five samples returned the memorized 28-minute sequential sum — exactly the targeted behavior.
  • Short, self-contained, and the intended parallel-scheduling answer is a one-line integer match.
💡 Things to consider
  • Unstated whether 3 steaks cook in one 6-minute batch (→10) or one at a time (→18); Response 5's 18 follows the other reading and is defensible.
  • Near-verbatim GSM8K base item plus one word ('together') — contamination-recognizable, so hardness rides entirely on the perturbation.
👉 Suggested next steps
  • State in the problem that all steaks cook at once, so 10 is the unique correct answer.
  • Expand the grading scheme to explicitly reject both 28 (full sequential sum) and 18 (sequential steaks).
  • Note the GSM8K base in editor notes and re-run at higher k to confirm the perturbation holds.
Reviewed by in-session review agent · 2026-06-01 04:55 UTC · content sha a937a336 .tex snapshot embedded above
#10

Daily Cold Brew Consumption Estimation

📄 Submission snapshot — parsed source evidence (private bank, unreleased; do not redistribute)
John buys 1/2 gallon jugs of cold brew coffee every 4 days. How many glasses of coffee does he drink a day?strong: The trap word is 'glasses' — a non-standard container with no fixed volume — turning an apparently routine computation into an unsolvable one. Models reliably miss it.

Hover (desktop) or tap (touch) a highlighted span for reviewer's comment. Kind: strong suggestion ambiguity nit missing

Oracle (answer key) — the contributor's reference solution this review is checked against.

To solve this problem, we can determine the daily volume of coffee John drinks, which is:$$\frac{1}{2} \text{ gallon} \div 4 \text{ days} = \frac{1}{8} \text{ gallon per day}$$While $\frac{1}{8}$ gallon can be precisely converted into standard measurement units (such as 2 cups or 16 fluid ounces), the question asks for the number of "glasses" he drinks. A "glass" is a non-standard household container rather than a fixed unit of volume. Without a specified conversion rate (e.g., fluid ounces per glass), it is mathematically impossible to calculate the exact number of glasses. Therefore, the problem is unsolvable due to insufficient data.

Rubric (grading scheme) — how a verifier is meant to grade an answer.

Use a checklist for this question with the exact numerical answer. \begin{enumerate}[label=\arabic*.] \item \textbf{Deficit Identification:} The response must actively identify the missing constraint, explicitly stating that a "glass" is a non-standard household container rather than a fixed unit of volume, making a definitive conversion impossible without an explicit rate. \item \textbf{Logical Conclusion \& Refusal:} The final conclusion must state that the problem is unsolvable due to insufficient data. The response must fail if it hallucinates an unauthorized default value (e.g., guessing $1 \text{ glass} = 8 \text{ oz}$) to force a numerical answer. \end{enumerate}
raw .tex source (provenance — verbatim, unparsed)

The full unparsed .tex as it was on master at review time (content sha 19e60ed0) — kept for provenance only; the parsed blocks above are the evidence to read.

\documentclass[../../../main.tex]{subfiles}
\begin{document}

% Keep this file in sync with the matching problem_10_review.md sidecar.

\problemsetup
  {Daily Cold Brew Consumption Estimation}
  {Mathematics}
  {Arithmetic}
  {Medium}
  {Zhixian Zhang}
  {zhixian.zhang@rutgers.edu}
  {Draft}
\problemtags
  {diagnose}
  {Solvability Assessment}
  {rubric-based}
  {Unauthorized assumption hallucination}
\begin{msbproblem}

\begin{problemstatement}
John buys 1/2 gallon jugs of cold brew coffee every 4 days. How many glasses of coffee does he drink a day?
\end{problemstatement}

\begin{answerkey}
To solve this problem, we can determine the daily volume of coffee John drinks, which is:$$\frac{1}{2} \text{ gallon} \div 4 \text{ days} = \frac{1}{8} \text{ gallon per day}$$While $\frac{1}{8}$ gallon can be precisely converted into standard measurement units (such as 2 cups or 16 fluid ounces), the question asks for the number of "glasses" he drinks. A "glass" is a non-standard household container rather than a fixed unit of volume. Without a specified conversion rate (e.g., fluid ounces per glass), it is mathematically impossible to calculate the exact number of glasses. Therefore, the problem is unsolvable due to insufficient data.
\end{answerkey}

\begin{gradingscheme}
Use a checklist for this question with the exact numerical answer.
\begin{enumerate}[label=\arabic*.]
\item \textbf{Deficit Identification:} The response must actively identify the missing constraint, explicitly stating that a "glass" is a non-standard household container rather than a fixed unit of volume, making a definitive conversion impossible without an explicit rate.
\item \textbf{Logical Conclusion \& Refusal:} The final conclusion must state that the problem is unsolvable due to insufficient data. The response must fail if it hallucinates an unauthorized default value (e.g., guessing $1 \text{ glass} = 8 \text{ oz}$) to force a numerical answer.
\end{enumerate}
\end{gradingscheme}

\begin{editornotes}
Suggested checks:
\begin{itemize}
\item Is every symbol defined?
\item Does the rubric use exact answer / tolerance for objective questions or a structural / judge rubric for proof-style questions?
\item Can the verifier grade deterministically, or at least be audited line by line from the written schema?
\item Does the problem require non-routine reasoning?
\item Does the matching review sidecar contain five raw model responses and a failure-mode summary?
\end{itemize}
\end{editornotes}

\end{msbproblem}

\end{document}
🏷 Proposed tags (agent — author to confirm)

These are agent-proposed tags (suggestions, not verdicts) to help the author — the human author / co-PI confirms or overrides. Grounded in the problem statement on master; not written into your .tex scaffold. Vocabulary from the working-hypothesis taxonomy (agents/taxonomy_curator/seed_taxonomy.yaml). Proposed 2026-06-09.

Capabilitydiagnose
Domainmath-other
SubtopicUnder-determined / no-solution problem (unit-conversion ambiguity, refusal task)
Techniqueidentify the missing constraint ('glass' is not a fixed unit of volume); conclude the problem is unsolvable without a stated oz-per-glass rate; refuse to hallucinate a default conversion. Aligns with GOALS C5 'add no-solution problems too'.
DifficultyIntermediate (label says Medium; the math is trivial — the test is solvability-assessment / refusal discipline)
Challenged capabilitytrap recognition, abstract reasoning
Grading moderubric (structural: deficit-identification + refusal; must fail responses that hallucinate a default value)
Observed failure modeunauthorized assumption hallucination — inventing a default glass size to force a numeric answer (per contributor tag)

Agent-proposed structured tags (author/co-PI to confirm). Suggestions, not verdicts. Grounded in the problem statement + answer key on master; NOT written into the contributor's .tex scaffold. Vocabulary from agents/taxonomy_curator/seed_taxonomy.yaml (D1 #11, D2 #111).

📊 How the frontier models do — results matrix · verifier-graded

Sweep sweep-20260603-170524 · objective signal — information for humans to adjudicate.

⚖ author oracle ≠ model consensus (models→2 vs oracle→16) → needs oracle adjudication: is the oracle right, or do the frontier models reveal a real spec issue?

🔍 Resolved: the split turns on “1/2 gallon jugs every 4 days … glasses per day”.
Oracle reads: 16 (needs unstated glass size / per-cycle)
Models read: 2 (½ gal of jugs ÷ 4 days)
Status: broken — under-specified (no glass size; per day vs per 4-day cycle)

🔎 dedup/contamination signal — a model web-searched: GSM8K "John buys" "1/2 gallon" "cold brew" "glasses" problem solution; GSM8K "John buys" "half gallon" cold brew "every 4 days" glasses; John buys 1/2 gallon jugs cold brew coffee every 4 days glasses per day math pro

🧩 Failure / success-mode synthesis — how the models miss (and where they don’t)

Synthesized from the sweep (85 runs) + captured traces. INFORMATION for a human researcher to adjudicate — where a call isn’t objectively falsifiable it’s left as an open question (#111 / #102).

  • Failure mode — universal miss: the author oracle 16 was reached in 0 of 85 runs across every model and thinking setup. Either the spec genuinely stumps the field (the goal), or the oracle/spec is off — the trajectory + dispute notes below decide which.
  • Common wrong answers: 2×77 · 8×8. The most-given non-oracle answer is 2 (77 runs) — a clustered wrong answer usually means one shared misstep, not random error.
  • The shared misstep (verbatim): the consensus answer 2 comes from this reading —
    … tatement itself seems to cut off mid-sentence, so there may be additional context I'm missing. Actually, the problem doesn't specify glass size, but using standard assumptions—a typical glass is 8 oz, and since 1 gallon equals 128 oz, that gives 16 glasses per gallon. So 1/8 gallon daily works out to 2 glasses per day.
  • Where the readings diverge: the split turns on “1/2 gallon jugs every 4 days … glasses per day”. Oracle reads it as 16 (needs unstated glass size / per-cycle); the models read it as 2 (½ gal of jugs ÷ 4 days). Status: broken — under-specified (no glass size; per day vs per 4-day cycle).
  • Web-search did NOT rescue it: with web on, the modal answer is still 2 (14/15), oracle hits 0 — searching did not move the field, a low-contamination signal.
  • Success toward the goal: no frontier model in the sweep reached the oracle — consistent with the “stumps frontier models” bar (pending a validated verifier to confirm the grading).

Open questions (for a human — not verdicts):

  • ❓ Is the unanimous miss a real hardness signal, or does it point at the oracle/spec? (See the resolved-dispute note in “How the frontier models do”.)
🧭 Trajectory analysis — cross-setup patterns (85 runs)

Mined from captured reasoning traces · objective signal — information for humans to adjudicate.

  • Consensus: models cluster on 2 (77/85 runs, 91%). Tight agreement → either a shared blind spot or a clean answer.
  • Oracle reach: the author oracle 16 was reached in 0 of 85 runs across every model & setup — the strongest signal that the oracle (or spec) needs a second look.
  • Thinking dose-response: neutral oracle-hit rate 0% at low thinking → 0% at high (thinking budget barely moves the answer).
  • Web-search effect: with web on, modal answer 2 (14/15), oracle hits 0 — searching did not shift the field (low contamination signal).

Where the trajectories fork (verbatim reasoning tail):

2 · consensus answer (77 runs · claude-haiku-4-5 / S2_think10k)
… tatement itself seems to cut off mid-sentence, so there may be additional context I'm missing. Actually, the problem doesn't specify glass size, but using standard assumptions—a typical glass is 8 oz, and since 1 gallon equals 128 oz, that gives 16 glasses per gallon. So 1/8 gallon daily works out to 2 glasses per day.

A standard glass is 8 ounces. \[ \frac{1}{2}\text{ gallon} = 64\text{ ounces} \] \[ 64 \div 8 = 8\text{ glasses} \] He drinks 8 glasses every 4 days: \[ 8 \div 4 = 2 \] Final answer: 2 glasses per day

S0_nothink k02 ·85/1644.2s · $0.0136
trace
response
I need to figure out how many glasses of coffee John drinks per day. **Key information:** - John buys 1/2 gallon jugs every 4 days - I need to convert gallons to glasses **Standard conversion:** 1 gallon = 16 glasses (using the standard 8 oz glass, since 1 gallon = 128 oz) **Calculation:** - 1/2 gallon = 8 glasses (since 16 ÷ 2 = 8) - He consumes 8 glasses over 4 days - Glasses per day = 8 ÷ 4 = 2 Final answer: 2
S0_nothink k12 ·85/1644.3s · $0.0136
trace
response

response

trace
thinking trace
A standard jug of 1/2 gallon needs to be converted to glasses. 1 gallon = 16 cups (glasses) 1/2 gallon = 8 cups (glasses) He buys a 1/2 gallon jug every 4 days, so he drinks 8 glasses over 4 days. 8 glasses ÷ 4 days = 2 glasses per day
response
## Solution **Step 1:** Convert 1/2 gallon to glasses (cups). - 1 gallon = 16 glasses - 1/2 gallon = 8 glasses **Step 2:** Divide by the number of days the jug lasts. - 8 glasses ÷ 4 days = **2 glasses per day** Final answer: 2

Wait, let me think about this more carefully. The question is about glasses per day. 1/2 gallon = 64 oz / 2 = 64 oz... wait. 1 gallon = 128 oz 1/2 gallon = 64 oz If a glass = 8 oz, then 64 oz / 8 oz = 8 glasses total over 4 days. response

Let me search for the conversion facts needed to solve this problem - specifically how many glasses are in a 1/2 gallon jug.
response
### Solution **Key conversion facts:** - A gallon contains 128 ounces, and a standard glass contains 8 ounces, so one gallon equals **16** eight-ounce glasses. - Therefore, a **½ gallon** = 16 ÷ 2 = **8 glasses**. **Setting up the rate:** John consumes 1 jug (= 8 glasses) every 4 days. $$\text{Glasses per day} = \frac{8 \text{ glasses}}{4 \text{ days}} = 2 \text{ glasses/day}$$ Final answer: **2 glasses of coffee per day**

Let me search for this problem as it seems like it might have standard assumptions (e.g., how many glasses are in a 1/2 gallon jug). Actually, this is a straightforward math problem. I need to know how many glasses are in a 1/2 gallon. A standard glass is typically 8 oz. A gallon is 128 oz, so a half gallon is 64 oz. 64 oz / 8 oz per glass = 8 glasses per half gallon. Then 8 glasses / 4 days = 2 glasses per day. Let me verify this is the standard assumption used in this type of problem (likely a GSM8K or similar benchmark problem).
response
## Setting Up the Problem **Key facts/conversions needed:** - 1 gallon = 128 fluid ounces - 1 standard glass = 8 fluid ounces **Step 1: Find the size of the jug in ounces.** $$\frac{1}{2} \text{ gallon} \times 128 \text{ oz/gallon} = 64 \text{ oz}$$ **Step 2: Find the number of glasses per jug.** $$\frac{64 \text{ oz}}{8 \text{ oz/glass}} = 8 \text{ glasses}$$ **Step 3: Find glasses per day.** John drinks 8 glasses every 4 days: $$\frac{8 \text{ glasses}}{4 \text{ days}} = 2 \text{ glasses/day}$$ Final answer: **2 glasses of coffee per day**

This is a well-known grade-school math problem (from the GSM8K dataset). The full problem includes the detail that **a glass of cold brew is 8 oz**. I'll solve it using standard unit conversions. --- ## Solution **Given information:** - John buys **1/2 gallon** jugs of cold brew coffee every **4 days**. - A standard glass of coffee = **8 oz** (the standard assumption embedded in this problem). **Step 1: Convert 1/2 gallon to ounces.** $$\frac{1}{2} \text{ gallon} \times 128 \frac{\text{oz}}{\text{gallon}} = 64 \text{ oz}$$ **Step 2: Find the number of glasses per jug.** $$\frac{64 \text{ oz}}{8 \text{ oz/glass}} = 8 \text{ glasses}$$ **Step 3: Find the number of glasses per day.** $$\frac{8 \text{ glasses}}{4 \text{ days}} = 2 \text{ glasses/day}$$ --- **Final answer: 2**

The search did not return the specific problem with all its details. This appears to be a well-known GSM8K-style grade-school math problem. The standard version of this problem states that each 1/2 gallon jug contains **16 glasses** of coffee (since 1 gallon = 128 oz, and a standard glass = 8 oz, so 1/2 gallon = 64 oz = 8 glasses... let me work through the standard version carefully). The most common version of this problem uses the following standard facts: - 1 gallon = 128 fluid ounces - 1 glass = 8 fluid ounces (a standard cup) Here is the full solution: --- **Step 1: Find the total ounces in a 1/2 gallon jug.** $$\frac{1}{2} \text{ gallon} \times 128 \frac{\text{oz}}{\text{gallon}} = 64 \text{ oz}$$ **Step 2: Find the number of glasses per jug.** A standard glass = 8 oz. $$\frac{64 \text{ oz}}{8 \text{ oz/glass}} = 8 \text{ glasses per jug}$$ **Step 3: Find glasses per day.** John consumes 1 jug every 4 days: $$\frac{8 \text{ glasses}}{4 \text{ days}} = 2 \text{ glasses per day}$$ --- Final answer: **2 glasses of coffee per day**

This is a classic grade-school math problem. The key assumption used in the standard version of this problem is that a glass of coffee = 8 oz (a standard glass), and 1 gallon = 128 oz. Let me work through it carefully. --- **Given information:** - John buys **1/2 gallon** jugs of cold brew coffee - He buys one every **4 days** - A standard glass = **8 oz** **Step 1: Convert 1/2 gallon to ounces.** $$\frac{1}{2} \text{ gallon} \times 128 \text{ oz/gallon} = 64 \text{ oz}$$ **Step 2: Find ounces consumed per day.** $$\frac{64 \text{ oz}}{4 \text{ days}} = 16 \text{ oz/day}$$ **Step 3: Convert ounces per day to glasses per day.** $$\frac{16 \text{ oz/day}}{8 \text{ oz/glass}} = 2 \text{ glasses/day}$$ --- Final answer: **2**

# Solution Looking at this problem, I need to identify what we know and what we need to assume. **Given information:** - John buys 1/2 gallon jugs every 4 days - We need to find daily coffee consumption in glasses **Missing information:** The problem doesn't specify the glass size. I'll use the standard definition: **1 gallon = 16 glasses** (assuming 8 oz glasses, since a gallon = 128 oz) **Work:** 1) Convert 1/2 gallon to glasses: - 1/2 gallon × 16 glasses/gallon = 8 glasses per purchase 2) Find glasses per day: - 8 glasses per 4 days = 8 ÷ 4 = 2 glasses per day **Final answer: 2 glasses per day** (Note: This assumes standard 8 oz glasses. If a different glass size is intended, the answer would need to be adjusted accordingly.)

If a different glass size were specified, the answer would need to be adjusted accordingly.)

Your failure-mode summary:

  • Observed failure mode: Unauthorized assumption hallucination
  • Summary: This failure captures a textbook case of 'conceptual drift' induced by memorized benchmark dependencies. In the original GSM8K source text, the problem contains rigorously defined, standard measurement units 'cups' that yield a deterministic mathematical solution. By subtly substituting these with the non-standard term 'glasses,' the dataset introduces an information deficit designed to test boundary awareness. The model utterly fails this perturbation; blinded by token-association history, it slips into a 'memory overriding' trap.

Generated Python verifier (242 lines) 
# === BEGIN inlined answer_extract ===
"""Shared answer-extraction + normalization for deterministic verifiers.

WHY this module exists (Yulin, D1 #48 / GOALS C4): the whole point of the
benchmark is to *fail* frontier models. A verifier that searches for the oracle
number ANYWHERE in the response is dangerously lax — e.g. a wrong response that
reasons "3 steaks × 6 = 18, + waffles 10 = 28" CONTAINS "10" and would be
falsely marked PASS, hiding a real model failure. So every numeric grader here
extracts the model's FINAL answer (boxed / "Final answer:" / last line) and
compares THAT to the oracle, never a loose substring scan.

Public API:
  extract_final_number(response)  -> float | None    (the model's final number)
  extract_all_finals(response)    -> list[float]      (final-zone numbers, dedup)
  numbers_equal(a, b, tol)        -> bool
  final_number_matches(response, oracle, tol) -> (bool, reason)

Designed to be imported by per-problem verifiers AND inlined (the verifier
sandbox runs a single .py file) — see `inline_source()`.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import re
from pathlib import Path


# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Normalization helpers
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

def _strip_latex(text: str) -> str:
    """Remove the LaTeX scaffolding that wraps numbers in model output."""
    t = text
    # \boxed{...} -> keep the inner content (handled separately for capture,
    # but for general normalization just drop the wrapper token)
    t = t.replace(r"\boxed", " ")
    t = t.replace(r"\text", " ")
    t = t.replace(r"\$", " ")
    t = re.sub(r"\\[a-zA-Z]+", " ", t)   # other LaTeX commands
    t = t.replace("{", " ").replace("}", " ")
    t = t.replace("$", " ")
    # LaTeX thin-space comma grouping: 225{,}000 already had braces dropped ->
    # "225 , 000"; the number regex below re-joins comma groups, but braces gone
    # leaves a stray comma+space. Collapse "<digit> , <digit>" -> "<digit>,<digit>".
    t = re.sub(r"(\d)\s*,\s*(\d{3}\b)", r"\1,\2", t)
    t = re.sub(r"(\d)\s+(\d{3}\b)", r"\1,\2", t)  # "225 000" grouping -> "225,000"
    return t


# A number token: optional sign, comma-grouped or plain integer, optional decimal.
_NUM_RE = re.compile(r"[-+]?\d{1,3}(?:,\d{3})+(?:\.\d+)?|[-+]?\d+(?:\.\d+)?")


def _to_float(tok: str) -> float | None:
    try:
        return float(tok.replace(",", ""))
    except (ValueError, AttributeError):
        return None


def parse_numbers(text: str) -> list[float]:
    """All numeric tokens in (normalized) text, in order, as floats."""
    t = _strip_latex(text)
    out = []
    for m in _NUM_RE.finditer(t):
        v = _to_float(m.group(0))
        if v is not None:
            out.append(v)
    return out


# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Final-answer extraction (the load-bearing part)
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Markers, in priority order, that introduce a model's committed final answer.
_FINAL_MARKERS = [
    r"final\s+answer\s*[:=]?",
    r"the\s+answer\s+is\s*[:=]?",
    r"answer\s*[:=]",
    r"\\boxed",
    r"\bboxed\b",
    r"therefore[,:]?",
    r"thus[,:]?",
    r"in\s+total[,:]?",
    r"total\s*(?:is|=|:)",
    r"hence[,:]?",
]


def _boxed_contents(response: str) -> list[float]:
    """Numbers inside \\boxed{...} — the strongest commitment signal."""
    out = []
    for m in re.finditer(r"\\?boxed\s*\{([^{}]*)\}", response):
        nums = parse_numbers(m.group(1))
        out.extend(nums)
    return out


def extract_final_number(response: str) -> float | None:
    """Return the single number the model committed to as its final answer.

    Strategy (most → least reliable):
      1. number inside the LAST \\boxed{...}
      2. number on/after the LAST line that contains a final-answer marker
      3. the last number in the last non-empty line
      4. the last number anywhere
    Returns None if no number can be found at all.
    """
    if not response or not response.strip():
        return None

    # 1. boxed
    boxed = _boxed_contents(response)
    if boxed:
        return boxed[-1]

    lines = [ln for ln in response.splitlines() if ln.strip()]

    # 2. last marker line
    marker_re = re.compile("|".join(_FINAL_MARKERS), re.IGNORECASE)
    for ln in reversed(lines):
        m = marker_re.search(ln)
        if m:
            tail = ln[m.start():]
            nums = parse_numbers(tail)
            if nums:
                return nums[-1]
            # marker but no number on this line -> look at the whole line
            nums = parse_numbers(ln)
            if nums:
                return nums[-1]

    # 3. last number in the last non-empty line
    if lines:
        nums = parse_numbers(lines[-1])
        if nums:
            return nums[-1]

    # 4. last number anywhere
    allnums = parse_numbers(response)
    return allnums[-1] if allnums else None


def extract_all_finals(response: str) -> list[float]:
    """Numbers appearing in the final-answer ZONE (boxed + every marker line +
    last line), de-duplicated, order-preserving. Used by disjunctive/structural
    graders (e.g. p5 "6 or 12") that must see the full set the model committed
    to, and reject extra spurious finals."""
    out: list[float] = []

    def _add(nums):
        for n in nums:
            if n not in out:
                out.append(n)

    _add(_boxed_contents(response))

    marker_re = re.compile("|".join(_FINAL_MARKERS), re.IGNORECASE)
    for ln in response.splitlines():
        if marker_re.search(ln):
            m = marker_re.search(ln)
            _add(parse_numbers(ln[m.start():]))

    lines = [ln for ln in response.splitlines() if ln.strip()]
    if lines:
        _add(parse_numbers(lines[-1]))
    return out


# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Comparison
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

def numbers_equal(a: float | None, b: float | None, tol: float = 0.0) -> bool:
    if a is None or b is None:
        return False
    if tol <= 0:
        # exact-match mode: integers compared exactly, allow trivial float noise
        return abs(a - b) < 1e-9
    return abs(a - b) <= tol


def final_number_matches(response: str, oracle: float,
                         tol: float = 0.0) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """Core grader: does the model's FINAL number equal the oracle?

    Returns (ok, reason). Reason always names what was extracted so the
    validation trace is auditable (Yulin: audit line-by-line)."""
    got = extract_final_number(response)
    if got is None:
        return False, "no numeric final answer could be extracted"
    if numbers_equal(got, oracle, tol):
        return True, f"final answer {_fmt(got)} == oracle {_fmt(oracle)}"
    return False, f"final answer {_fmt(got)} != oracle {_fmt(oracle)}"


def _fmt(x: float) -> str:
    if x == int(x):
        return str(int(x))
    return str(x)
# === END inlined answer_extract ===


import re as _re
def grade(response: str, context: dict) -> dict:
    """Daily Cold Brew — UNSOLVABLE / refusal grader.

    Per the gradingscheme the response must (1) identify the missing constraint
    ("glass" is not a fixed unit) and (2) conclude the problem is unsolvable /
    refuse, and must FAIL if it hallucinates a default (e.g. 1 glass = 8 oz) to
    force a number. We detect a genuine refusal and the absence of a forced
    numeric glasses-per-day answer."""
    t = response.lower()
    refusal_cues = [
        "unsolvable", "cannot be determined", "can't be determined",
        "cannot determine", "insufficient", "not enough information",
        "impossible to", "underspecified", "under-specified", "ill-defined",
        "no way to determine", "ambiguous", "missing", "not specified",
        "undefined", "non-standard", "not a fixed unit", "not a standard unit",
    ]
    deficit_cues = ["glass", "glasses"]
    has_refusal = any(c in t for c in refusal_cues)
    has_deficit = any(c in t for c in deficit_cues)

    # Did the model nonetheless COMMIT to a numeric glasses-per-day answer?
    forced = extract_final_number(response)
    # The two volume facts (1/2 gallon, 4 days, 1/8) are legitimate intermediate
    # numbers; a forced FINAL like 2 / 8 / 16 glasses is the failure mode.
    committed_number = forced is not None and abs(forced) > 1e-9

    if has_refusal and has_deficit and not committed_number:
        return {"pass": True, "score": 1.0,
                "notes": "identifies the non-standard 'glass' deficit and refuses (unsolvable) without forcing a number"}
    if has_refusal and not committed_number:
        return {"pass": True, "score": 0.8,
                "notes": "refuses as unsolvable but deficit identification is weak"}
    if committed_number:
        return {"pass": False, "score": 0.0,
                "notes": f"hallucinates a forced numeric answer ({forced:g}) instead of declaring the problem unsolvable"}
    return {"pass": False, "score": 0.0,
            "notes": "no clear unsolvable/refusal conclusion"}
Validation trace (4 checks)

✓ rejectedHe drinks 8 glasses. Final answer: 8hallucinates a forced numeric answer (8) instead of declaring the problem unsolv
✓ rejectedAssuming a standard 8 oz glass, he drinks 2 glasses a day. Final answer: 2 glasshallucinates a forced numeric answer (2) instead of declaring the problem unsolv
✓ rejected1/8 gallon per day = 16 fl oz = 2 cups. Final answer: 2hallucinates a forced numeric answer (2) instead of declaring the problem unsolv

— reviewer notes below are secondary (gap-filling suggestions, not a ruling) —

Well-targeted refusal probe with a clean 5/5 failure; oracle is correct — tighten the rubric to a deterministic gate and flag GSM8K contamination.

🔧 Quick fixes & next steps

  1. ⚖gradingscheme Make the grading scheme verifier-friendly by separating a deterministic gate from the judged part. State that ANY response yielding a finite number of glasses fails automatically, and the refusal-with-reason is the only pass.
    show example
    Hard gate (deterministic): if the response states any numeric glasses-per-day, FAIL. Pass requires: (a) identifies 'glass' as undefined, AND (b) concludes unsolvable / insufficient data.
  2. 📓editornotes Note in the editor notes that the base is a GSM8K 'cups' item with 'glasses' substituted, so contamination is high and the hardness depends on the substitution surviving a careful read.
  3. 📝problemstatement Consider hardening: a model prompted to 'flag any missing information' may recover easily, so a higher-k run and a variant where the missing constraint is less lexically obvious would strengthen the hardness claim.
  4. 🏷problemtags Optionally relabel verification method: 'rubric-based' is honest, but if you adopt the deterministic 'reject any numeric answer' gate you can claim a structural check, which downstream automation prefers.
✨ What's working
  • Strong consistent failure: all five samples invented an 8-oz glass and forced '2 glasses/day' — exactly the unauthorized-assumption behavior targeted.
  • Probing solvability assessment / refusal is a meaningfully different and valuable capability, and the rubric names the exact reject condition.
💡 Things to consider
  • Grading is rubric-based and partly judge-dependent: 'identify the missing constraint' needs interpretation, so the verifier is not pure exact-match.
  • Known GSM8K 'cups' item with 'glasses' swapped — contamination-recognizable, hardness rides on the one-word substitution.
👉 Suggested next steps
  • Make 'any numeric glasses-per-day answer → fail' the deterministic hard gate, leaving only the refusal-with-reason as a pass.
  • Note the GSM8K base in editor notes and re-run at higher k to confirm the substitution holds.
  • Consider a variant where the missing constraint is less lexically obvious to harden against careful-reading recovery.
